entry.title: Combined summary - Lehar / Parlour Paper
entry.summary: The academic paper titled ["Market Power and the Bitcoin Protocol"](http://ewfs.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/tx_fees-18.pdf) challenges the conventional view that Bitcoin miners always aim to maximize transaction fees for each block they mine. It introduces the concept of strategic behavior among miners, where they might intentionally avoid maximizing fees in the short term to potentially influence average transaction fees over time, thus increasing them. This notion suggests a more nuanced economic interaction within the Bitcoin ecosystem than previously understood, implying miners may prioritize long-term gains over short-term profits. This perspective invites a reevaluation of miner incentives and opens up new avenues for research on the economic strategies underpinning the Bitcoin protocol. The paper also sheds light on the technical aspects of how blocks are considered "full," contrasting the observed practices with the theoretical settings in Bitcoin's code. Through empirical evidence, it questions the alignment between miners' actions and the optimization of fee income on a per-block basis. Furthermore, it discusses the criteria for "priority violations" and examines the occurrence of stale or orphan blocks, suggesting that the paper's conclusions about miner behavior may not be entirely supported by the data presented. Another focal point is the investigation into the prevalence of empty blocks and their timing relative to preceding blocks. Historical analysis reveals a significant decrease in the frequency of empty blocks and a pattern that contradicts the hypothesis of latency issues being a primary cause for their occurrence. Instead, the findings indicate that most empty blocks are mined very shortly after their preceding block, challenging assumptions about miners' motivations and strategies for block creation. This discourse contributes to the broader conversation about the dynamics of the Bitcoin network and mining behaviors. By questioning established beliefs and presenting empirical analyses, the paper encourages further examination and discussion regarding the complexities of economic decision-making within the cryptocurrency domain.
